nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or seasonally flooded freshwater margins on Caribbean islands; the population popularized in the aquarium hobby was collected west of Havana, Cuba

decorPreferences: fine, smooth substrate to anchor delicate roots
open foreground with strong direct light

avoidWith: boisterous fish that uproot fine carpet plants

summary: Dwarf Baby Tears (Hemianthus callitrichoides) is a miniature carpeting plant from Cuba, producing a dense mat of millimetre-sized bright-green leaves across the aquarium foreground. It is generally considered one of the more demanding aquarium plants, tending to require strong light, pressurised CO2, and a nutrient-rich substrate to thrive.
careNotes:
Dwarf Baby Tears (Hemianthus callitrichoides) — known in the hobby almost universally as 'HC' or 'HC Cuba' — is a small creeping herb native to the Caribbean, popularised in the aquarium trade by Takashi Amano around 2003 and widely regarded as a benchmark foreground carpet plant. The species is now more formally classified under the name Micranthemum callitrichoides, though the older Hemianthus callitrichoides remains dominant in retail and hobby usage; the family is Linderniaceae, though older references often still list Scrophulariaceae. It is among the smallest aquarium plants available, with individual leaves only 3–5 mm across on a carpet that typically stays 3–5 cm tall. It should not be confused with Hemianthus micranthemoides ('Pearl Weed'), which is a much larger stem plant with a very different growth habit.

Light is best kept at the high end — bright, direct lighting is needed to keep the carpet growing compactly and horizontally rather than becoming leggy and sparse. CO2 injection is strongly recommended, with pressurised systems supplying roughly 15–25 mg/L cited as the target; liquid carbon supplements offer only limited success and are rarely sufficient on their own. Preferred water parameters are 21–27 °C, pH 6.0–7.3, and a general hardness of around 4–10 dGH, with soft to moderately soft water generally preferred. A fine, smooth, nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) is generally advised, with iron-rich fertilisation and steady column dosing to support healthy growth; the plant's tiny, delicate roots make planting fiddly, and a 'dry start' method — growing the plant emersed on damp substrate before the tank is flooded — is widely used to allow it to anchor firmly before submerged conditions begin.

Once a carpet has established, trimming every 2–3 weeks helps keep the lower leaves illuminated; neglected carpets tend to detach at the base and float up in sheets. The plant is sensitive to algae outbreaks if light, CO2, nutrients, or maintenance slip out of balance, so stability across all parameters matters more than aggressive optimisation. Dwarf Baby Tears is often a centrepiece of iwagumi and nature-style aquascapes; Micranthemum 'Monte Carlo' is a frequently recommended alternative for those whose setup cannot maintain the conditions this species requires.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative: the carpet spreads horizontally by short runners, and aquascapers expand coverage by lifting a mature mat, dividing it into small clumps of a few stems each, and replanting them a few centimetres apart across the foreground. New growth with attached roots can also be trimmed and replanted. The dry start method (growing emersed on damp substrate under a sealed lid for 4–8 weeks before flooding) is widely used to establish a dense, well-rooted carpet before submerged conditions begin.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica plant details for Micranthemum (Hemianthus) callitrichoides 'Cuba'. Provided height (3–5+ cm), high light demand (>0.5 W/L), high CO2 (~15–25 mg/L), medium growth rate, foreground placement, Cuban origin (west of Havana), and 'advanced' care rating. The originally supplied URL (.../Hemianthus-callitrichoides) 404'd; resolved to this versioned product page via Tropica's search.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked carpet placement, mm-scale leaves, moderate-to-high light, CO2 strongly recommended, medium-to-fast growth, temperature 68–82 °F (~20–28 °C), Cuban origin, and the planting technique of separating a tissue-culture portion into individual plugs spaced apart for horizontal spread.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (listed under older Scrophulariaceae), origin (Cuba/North America), height ~1–2 in, medium-to-high light, CO2 required, pH 6.0–7.5, moderate-to-fast growth, medium-to-difficult care, and the dry-start recommendation for anchoring delicate roots.

additional.name: 2HR Aquarist
additional.notes: Used (via search snippet — page returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch) for the widely cited '95% failure rate without CO2' framing, the bright-lighting requirement for compact horizontal growth, the importance of fine nutrient-rich substrate with iron, and the trim-every-three-weeks maintenance cadence.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 21–27 °C, pH 6.0–7.3, hardness 5–11 dGH, maximum height 0–5 cm, foreground carpeting placement, propagation by runners, and substrate/iron fertilisation guidance. Used to average water parameters with Buce Plant (pH) and Tropica/Modern Aquarium (temperature).
